bibliographic_data.title: Differential Parity: Relative Fairness Between Two Sets of Decisions
bibliographic_data.abstract: With AI systems widely applied to assist human in decision-making processes such as talent hiring, school admission, and loan approval; there is an increasing need to ensure that the decisions made are fair. One major challenge for analyzing fairness in decisions is that the standards are highly subjective and contextual—there is no consensus for what absolute fairness means for every scenario. Not to say that different fairness standards often conflict with each other. To bypass this issue, this work aims to test relative fairness in decisions. That is, instead of defining what are “absolutely” fair decisions, we propose to test the relative fairness of one decision set against another with differential parity— the difference between two sets of decisions should be independent from a certain sensitive attribute. This proposed differential parity fairness notion has the following benefits: (1) it avoids the ambiguous and contradictory definition of ‘absolutely’ fair decisions; (2) it reveals the relative preference and bias between two decision sets; (3) differential parity can serve as a new group fairness notion when a reference set of decisions (ground truths) is provided. One limitation for differential parity is that, it requires the two sets of decisions under comparison to be made on the same data subjects. To overcome this limitation, we propose to utilize a machine learning model to bridge the gap between the two decisions sets made on different data and estimate the differential parity.

core_content.novelty_claim: Proposes a new fairness notion called differential parity to test relative fairness between two decision sets, avoiding ambiguous definitions of absolute fairness and revealing the relative preference or bias.
core_content.key_methodology: The method involves comparing two sets of decisions by ensuring their difference is independent from a sensitive attribute. For cases where direct comparison isn't possible due to different data subjects, a machine learning model is used to estimate differential parity.
outcomes: Introduces the concept of differential parity as a new fairness notion that avoids ambiguous definitions and reveals relative preferences or biases between decision sets.
Develops an estimation framework for differential parity when decisions are made by different individuals on different data.
core_content.full_text_markdown: In recent years, AI systems have been widely used in decision-making processes such as talent hiring, school admission, and loan approval. Ensuring fairness in these decisions is crucial, but the standards for absolute fairness can be highly subjective and context-dependent. This paper proposes a new approach called differential parity to test relative fairness between two sets of decisions. Instead of defining what are 'absolutely' fair decisions, this method compares one decision set against another by ensuring their difference is independent from a certain sensitive attribute. The benefits include avoiding ambiguous definitions of absolute fairness, revealing the relative preference or bias between decision sets, and serving as a new group fairness notion when a reference set (ground truth) is provided. A limitation is that differential parity requires decisions to be made on the same data subjects. To overcome this, the paper proposes using a machine learning model to estimate differential parity even when decisions are made by different individuals on different data.
